question.label: Landing check-in
question.name: What's been most useful for you in week two, Riley?
question.description: Opens with a calm read on what is helping him settle in without implying trouble.

answer: I am comfortable reading the services, but I am less sure how decisions are really made here.
note: He sounds engaged with the codebase but volunteered a clarity gap about how decisions are made here, which is off-signature and should be picked up next.

question.label: Decision-making map
question.name: When you say 'how decisions are really made here,' what feels least clear?
question.description: Drills into his specific uncertainty about decision-making norms before moving on.

answer: I can trace most request paths now, though I still miss historical context behind certain tradeoffs.
note: He has the technical request-path map but still lacks historical context behind tradeoffs, which is a mild remaining clarity gap.

question.label: Tradeoff context
question.name: Which tradeoffs feel hardest to understand without the backstory?
question.description: Drills into the specific missing historical context so the manager can remove that orientation gap.

answer: I have not been fully clear on who is on point for each domain when priorities conflict.
note: He names a specific clarity gap: unclear ownership by domain when priorities conflict. [THREAD-DEFERRED] Ownership-by-domain confusion should be picked up next session if still live.

question.label: Context sources
question.name: When you need context before making a code decision, where do you go first?
question.description: Shows whether he knows the right people and channels for architecture context.

answer: I am trying not to interrupt people, so sometimes I keep questions in a notes doc instead of asking.
note: He is withholding questions to avoid interrupting people, which strongly signals unclear onboarding channels and a mild growth drag from not seeking context directly.

question.label: Notes doc habit
question.name: That notes doc habit — what makes a question feel interruptive to ask?
question.description: Drills into the specific hesitation that is stopping him from asking for context in real time.

answer: I would benefit from a clearer guide for what to escalate quickly versus what to batch.
note: Deficiency-as-request: he names a missing escalation guide, signaling strong clarity gap and mild friction/stress around asking in real time.

question.label: Support network
question.name: Who should be part of your go-to support circle here?
question.description: Identifies the relationships that should be intentionally built next to keep onboarding moving.

answer: The runbook helped, but some practical handoff details only show up during live incidents.
note: He names a concrete onboarding gap: practical handoff details are only learned during live incidents, signaling mild uncertainty and friction rather than a clear support network.

question.label: Live incidents gap
question.name: Those live incidents — which handoff details are still hardest to see ahead of time?
question.description: Drills into the specific incident handoff gaps so support can be made concrete.

answer: A short weekly architecture review with someone senior would help me calibrate faster.
note: Names a missing support structure — regular senior architecture review — which signals an ongoing clarity gap; [THREAD-DEFERRED-WINDDOWN] weekly architecture review with someone senior.

question.label: Next support
question.name: What would make the next two weeks easier for you?
question.description: Closes by turning any missing context into a concrete support request for the next stretch.

answer: If I know exactly what ownership I should take this month, I can contribute with more confidence.
note: Names missing ownership clarity as what would make the next stretch easier, a deficiency-as-request that signals mild strain and clear role ambiguity.

question.label: Priority ranking
question.name: If you had to cut your work in half tomorrow, what would you drop first — and what's non-negotiable?
question.description: Forces a real priority ranking. Vague answers are a clarity signal.

note: Skipped the priority-ranking question, so this turn provides no new clarity or engagement signal.
